title: F. J. Thwaites

infobox.value: Frederick Joseph Thwaites  (1908-05-23)23 May 1908  [Balmain, New South Wales](/wiki/Balmain,_New_South_Wales "Balmain, New South Wales"), Australia

(23 May 1908 – 13 August 1979) was an Australian novelist whose books sold over four million copies. He was best known for his first work *[The Broken Melody](/wiki/The_Broken_Melody_(novel) "The Broken Melody (novel)")*, which was adapted into a [1938 film](/wiki/The_Broken_Melody_(1937_film) "The Broken Melody (1937 film)").[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-broken-2)

['BOY'S AMBITION REALISED How 'Broken Melody' Was Written', *The Courier-Mail (Brisbane)*, Tuesday 5 July 1938 p 10](http://nla.gov.au/nla.news-article41004420?searchTerm=%22the%20broken%20melody%22%20cinesound&searchLimits=l-category=Article%7Ccategory%3AArticle%7C%7C%7Csortby=dateAsc)

["NOVELS FOR SCREEN"](http://nla.gov.au/nla.news-article104215440). *[The Land](/wiki/The_Land_(newspaper) "The Land (newspaper)")*. Sydney: National Library of Australia. 3 March 1933. p. 3. Retrieved 30 October 2014.

["Travellers in the Strathaird"](http://nla.gov.au/nla.news-article85178787). *[The Daily News](/wiki/Daily_News_(Perth,_Western_Australia) "Daily News (Perth, Western Australia)")*. Perth: National Library of Australia. 5 December 1933. p. 5 Edition: LATE CITY. Retrieved 30 October 2014.

["AUSTRALIAN NOVELIST"](http://nla.gov.au/nla.news-article99240274). *[Narromine News and Trangie Advocate](/wiki/Narromine_News_and_Trangie_Advocate "Narromine News and Trangie Advocate")*. NSW: National Library of Australia. 26 January 1934. p. 4. Retrieved 30 October 2014.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Australian novelist (1908–1979)

| F. J. Thwaites | |
| --- | --- |
| Born | Frederick Joseph Thwaites  (1908-05-23)23 May 1908  [Balmain, New South Wales](/wiki/Balmain,_New_South_Wales "Balmain, New South Wales"), Australia |
| Died | 13 August 1979(1979-08-13) (aged 71) |
| Occupation | Novelist |
| Spouse | [Jessica Harcourt](/wiki/Jessica_Harcourt "Jessica Harcourt")  ​ (m. 1938)​ |
| Children | 2 |

**Frederick Joseph Thwaites** (23 May 1908 – 13 August 1979) was an Australian novelist whose books sold over four million copies. He was best known for his first work *[The Broken Melody](/wiki/The_Broken_Melody_(novel) "The Broken Melody (novel)")*, which was adapted into a [1938 film](/wiki/The_Broken_Melody_(1937_film) "The Broken Melody (1937 film)").[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-broken-2)

## Biography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=F._J._Thwaites&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Biography")]

Born in the inner Sydney suburb of [Balmain](/wiki/Balmain,_New_South_Wales "Balmain, New South Wales"), Thwaites left school at the age of 13 and originally worked in the clothing industry. He wrote *The Broken Melody* when he was 19; it was originally self-published but eventually became a best seller, selling 55,000 copies in Australia and 25,000 in England.[[3]](#cite_note-Courier-3)

In February 1933, Thwaites left for England to write screen scenarios for Elstree Studios.[[4]](#cite_note-4) He spent several months there, and several months in Hollywood, attempting to sell his work to movie producers. Thwaites said on his return:

> I spent four months in Hollywood but had bad luck with my novels because of copyright laws. It is absolutely essential that after publication of a book in Australia and before three months has passed it has been copyrighted in Washington. I failed to do that, and I have no doubt it cost me the loss of the three pictures, for the picture folk only make pictures of stories that have been out twelve months on the American market and are protected, whereas my stories were open for any American publisher to copy without paying a cent.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

He later estimated this cost him £15,000.[[6]](#cite_note-perth-6)

On his return to Australia in late 1933, Thwaites then toured the country looking for manuscripts to publish.[[7]](#cite_note-7) In 1936, he founded his own publishing firm, The F. J. Thwaites Publishing Co.[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[9]](#cite_note-9)

In May 1937, Thwaites joined Adelaide radio station 5KA as production manager. During this time he met [Jessica Harcourt](/wiki/Jessica_Harcourt "Jessica Harcourt"), who became his wife.[[10]](#cite_note-10) She later recalled:

> he wouldn't hear of my being an actress any more, so with great regret I left the career I really loved... we travelled all over the world. I was his secretary and note-taker and diary keeper and so on. That was all excitng, but it didn't do for me what I wanted. I wanted a career in movies... He was a marvellous husband and a marvellous writer.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

In 1938, Thwaites and his wife left for England via the Pacific Islands and Panama Canal, researching material for novels. They intended to stay in England for three years but World War II saw them return to Australia by the end of 1939.[[12]](#cite_note-12)

Thwaites was one of the most popular Australian authors of the 1940s and 1950s, with many of his works being adapted for radio.

In late 1954, Thwaites set up a company in England, Harcourt Press, to publish his books there.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

Thwaites was married to actor Jessica Harcourt from 1938 until his death and together they had two sons.[[14]](#cite_note-Thwaites-14)

Much of his work was set in the Riverina region. His great-grandfather was Frederick Jenkins, who started Buckinbong Station.[[3]](#cite_note-Courier-3)

In the late 1960s, Thwaites was financing and managing housing projects.[[15]](#cite_note-times-15)

## Adaptations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=F._J._Thwaites&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Adaptations")]

Thwaites was no fan of the Australian film industry. According to a 1933 interview:

> He deplored the presentation abroad of such films as *On Our Selection*, *Harmony Row*, *The Sentimental Bloke*, and *The Squatter's Daughter*, in all of which there was at least one imbecile or half-wit. People abroad viewing these pictures could not be blamed for coming to the conclusion that about one person in every four or five in Australia was sub normal. The [Efftee Studios](/wiki/Efftee_Studios "Efftee Studios") in Australia were deserving of praise for their pioneering work, but surely it was possible to portray humour on the screen without associating it with lunacy.[[6]](#cite_note-perth-6)

*[The Broken Melody](/wiki/The_Broken_Melody_(1938_film) "The Broken Melody (1938 film)")* was turned into an Australian film in 1938, directed by [Ken G. Hall](/wiki/Ken_G._Hall "Ken G. Hall"), who had made *[On Our Selection](/wiki/On_Our_Selection_(1932_film) "On Our Selection (1932 film)")* and *[The Squatter's Daughter](/wiki/The_Squatter%27s_Daughter_(1933_film) "The Squatter's Daughter (1933 film)")*.

In 1935, it was announced that *Broken Wings* and *Flames of Convention* were to be filmed in England but that did not happen.[[16]](#cite_note-16) In 1937, *The Defender* was going to be adapted by [National Studios](/w/index.php?title=National_Studios&action=edit&redlink=1 "National Studios (page does not exist)"), but that was not done either.[[17]](#cite_note-17)

## Reception

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=F._J._Thwaites&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Reception")]

According to Ron Blaber (in the *[Australian Dictionary of Biography](/wiki/Australian_Dictionary_of_Biography "Australian Dictionary of Biography")*) although Thwaites was: "seen by critics as romantic and sentimental, his thirty-one adventure novels—characterized by themes of struggle and redemption—were immensely popular, particularly with lending libraries, and sold more than four million copies. Written in 'tempestuous prose', they were crammed with incident and swung between Australian and exotic settings".[[14]](#cite_note-Thwaites-14)

## Writings

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=F._J._Thwaites&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Writings")]

* *[The Broken Melody](/wiki/The_Broken_Melody_(novel) "The Broken Melody (novel)")* (1930)
* *[Hell's Doorway](/wiki/Hell%27s_Doorway "Hell's Doorway")* (1932)
* *[Flames of Convention](/wiki/Flames_of_Convention "Flames of Convention")* (1933)
* *[Where Gods Are Vain](/wiki/Where_Gods_Are_Vain "Where Gods Are Vain")* (1934)
* *[Broken Wings](/wiki/Broken_Wings_(Thwaites_novel) "Broken Wings (Thwaites novel)")* (1934)[[18]](#cite_note-18)
* *[The Melody Lingers](/wiki/The_Melody_Lingers "The Melody Lingers")* (1935) – sequel to *The Broken Melody*
* *[The Mad Doctor](/wiki/The_Mad_Doctor_(novel) "The Mad Doctor (novel)")* (1935) – a doctor works in Africa and invents a cure for paralysis[[19]](#cite_note-19)
* *[The Defender](/wiki/The_Defender_(Thwaites_novel) "The Defender (Thwaites novel)")* (1936)[[20]](#cite_note-20)
* *[The Redemption](/wiki/The_Redemption_(novel) "The Redemption (novel)")* (1936)[[21]](#cite_note-21)
* *[Rock End](/wiki/Rock_End "Rock End")* (1937)
* *[A Man of Destiny](/wiki/A_Man_of_Destiny "A Man of Destiny")* (1938)
* *[The Mad Doctor in Harley Street](/wiki/The_Mad_Doctor_in_Harley_Street "The Mad Doctor in Harley Street")* (1938)
* *[Fever](/wiki/Fever_(Thwaites_novel) "Fever (Thwaites novel)")* (1939)
* *[Whispers in Tahiti](/wiki/Whispers_in_Tahiti "Whispers in Tahiti")* (1940)
* *[Wind in the Bracken](/wiki/Wind_in_the_Bracken "Wind in the Bracken")* (1941)
* *[Shadows Over Rangoon](/wiki/Shadows_Over_Rangoon "Shadows Over Rangoon")* (1941)[[22]](#cite_note-22)
* *[Out of the Dawn](/wiki/Out_of_the_Dawn "Out of the Dawn")* (1945)[[23]](#cite_note-23)
* *[They Lived That Spring](/wiki/They_Lived_That_Spring "They Lived That Spring")* (1946)
* *[The Night Closed Down](/wiki/The_Night_Closed_Down "The Night Closed Down")* (1948)
* *[Oasis of Shalimar](/wiki/Oasis_of_Shalimar "Oasis of Shalimar")* (1950)
* *[The Dark Abyss](/wiki/The_Dark_Abyss "The Dark Abyss")* (1951)
* *[Roof Over Heaven](/wiki/Roof_Over_Heaven "Roof Over Heaven")* (1953)
* *[That Was the Hour](/wiki/That_Was_the_Hour "That Was the Hour")* (1956)
* *[White Moonlight](/wiki/White_Moonlight "White Moonlight")* (1957)
* *[Husky Be My Guide](/wiki/Husky_Be_My_Guide "Husky Be My Guide")* (1957) – travel book
* *[No Rainbow in the Sky](/wiki/No_Rainbow_in_the_Sky "No Rainbow in the Sky")* (1959)
* *[Press on Regardless](/wiki/Press_on_Regardless_(travel_book) "Press on Regardless (travel book)")* (1960) – travel book
* *[Beyond the Rainbow](/wiki/Beyond_the_Rainbow_(novel) "Beyond the Rainbow (novel)")* (1961)
* *[Destination Spain](/wiki/Destination_Spain "Destination Spain")* (1962) – travel book
* *[A Mountain for Monique](/wiki/A_Mountain_for_Monique "A Mountain for Monique")* (1964)
* *[Shall Come a Time](/wiki/Shall_Come_a_Time "Shall Come a Time")* (1967)
* *[Sky Full of Thunder](/wiki/Sky_Full_of_Thunder "Sky Full of Thunder")* (1968)
* *[No Love to Give](/wiki/No_Love_to_Give "No Love to Give")* (1969)
* *[Tracks I Knew Not](/wiki/Tracks_I_Knew_Not "Tracks I Knew Not")* (1973)
